issues.description: The narration claims biomagnification 'ties to the ten-percent rule: less biomass up top carrying the same toxin means higher concentration.' This is a real but subtle and partly misleading mechanistic claim. The 10% rule is about ENERGY transfer, not toxin mass conservation. The toxin does not stay constant in total mass; concentration rises mainly because a predator eats MANY contaminated prey and cannot excrete the fat-soluble toxin, so dose accumulates per gram of predator tissue. Invoking the 10% rule as the cause risks a student writing on the exam that 'biomagnification happens because only 10% of toxin transfers,' which is wrong (toxin is RETAINED, not 90% lost like energy). The diagram numbers (0.01 to 10 ppm) are good and concrete, but the energy-rule framing should be softened to a loose analogy, not a causal mechanism.
issues.suggested_fix: Reframe as: concentration rises because each predator eats many prey and stores (cannot excrete) the toxin — the opposite of the 10% energy rule, where energy is LOST up the chain while toxin is RETAINED and concentrated. Avoid implying the 10% rule causes biomagnification.

issues.description: Narration says concentration 'multiplies at every step' and 'can be a thousandfold higher,' but the diagram shows ~10x per step over 4 levels = 1000x total. Consistent, but a reasonable student may conflate the per-step factor (~10x) with the total (~1000x). Minor clarity risk only.
issues.suggested_fix: State explicitly: '~10x per trophic level, roughly 1000x from producer to top predator across this four-level chain.'

issues.description: The light/dark logic is stated correctly (light = NPP net, dark = R, GPP = NPP + R) but an unstated assumption is hidden: the method assumes respiration in the light bottle equals respiration in the dark bottle. That equality is WHY you can add the dark-bottle R back to the light-bottle net to recover GPP. If a sharp student asks 'why is dark-bottle respiration the same as light-bottle respiration?' the outline never answers, and the GPP = NPP + R step looks like an assertion rather than a consequence. The INITIAL bottle's role is also under-explained — it is the baseline DO that both other readings are measured against.
issues.suggested_fix: Add one line: 'We assume respiration is the same in both bottles, so the respiration measured in the dark can be added back to the light bottle's net to recover gross production.' Briefly note the initial bottle sets the DO baseline.

issues.description: Mechanism is excellent and the 'not poison, not CO2 depletion' trap is well handled. One small omission: many blooms also cause oxygen swings via nighttime respiration of the living algae themselves (and surface mats blocking light to submerged plants) BEFORE mass die-off. Not required by the CED, and adding it could muddy the clean decomposer-O2 message, so this is optional only.
issues.suggested_fix: Optional: a one-line aside that decomposition of dead algae is the dominant O2 sink; no change needed if brevity is preferred.

issues.description: Coral bleaching is described as corals expelling symbiotic algae due to heat AND acidification stress. The dominant, CED-emphasized driver of bleaching is THERMAL stress; acidification primarily impairs calcification/skeleton-building rather than directly causing the algal expulsion event. Lumping acidification as a co-cause of bleaching is a mild oversimplification.
issues.suggested_fix: Clarify that thermal stress is the primary bleaching trigger, while acidification mainly weakens the calcium-carbonate skeleton — both harm reefs but via different routes.

issues.description: Phosphorus 'no atmosphere' contrast with C and N is made explicit, correct, and directly addresses the AP trap. Reuse (not re-teach) of M14 carbon/nitrogen is appropriate.
Bioaccumulation (within one organism, over time) vs biomagnification (between trophic levels, up the chain) is crisply and correctly distinguished.

The pause genuinely tests the concept: it forces separate computation of NPP, R, and GPP with the relationships, and the worked solution (14) explains the reasoning and includes a GPP>NPP sanity check rather than just stating answers.

overall_assessment: Solid, exam-aligned module. The four CED traps (P no-atmosphere, eutrophication = O2 depletion not poison, biomagnification vs bioaccumulation, light/dark GPP=NPP+R) are all handled correctly and the pause question truly tests the concept with worked reasoning. Two fixable gaps: (1) the biomagnification narration mis-attributes the cause to the 10% energy rule — toxin is RETAINED/concentrated, the opposite of energy being lost, and this framing could seed a real misconception; (2) the productivity lab leaves the 'equal respiration in both bottles' assumption implicit, making GPP=NPP+R look asserted rather than derived. Minor refinements to coral bleaching (thermal-primary) and per-step vs total magnification factor would tighten it further. No critical misconception is taught outright; verdict minor.
